mechanism_of_action: Killing of Escherichia coli by the myeloperoxidase system was due to the oxidation of chloride ion to hypochlorous acid (HOCl), followed by the reaction of HOCl with bacterial components. Amine and/or amide components of the bacteria convert HOCl to nitrogen-chlorine (N-Cl) derivatives. These N-Cl derivatives are powerful oxidizing agents and appeared responsible for increased killing during prolonged incubations. The mechanism involves oxidation of bacterial sulfhydryls, oxidation of other easily oxidized bacterial components, chlorination of bacterial components to yield C-Cl derivatives, internal oxidation of N-Cl derivatives, and fragmentation/cleavage of peptide or amide bonds, contributing to release of peptides from the bacterial cell envelope and disrupting bacterial structure and inactivating essential enzymes.
